file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# instrument-review.sh — Run instrument suite against a commission.
#
# Runs quality scorers (blind + aware) and optionally the codebase
# integration scorer via the generic instrument runner.
#
# Usage:
#   ./bin/instrument-review.sh --commission <id> --repo <path> [options]
#
# Options:
#   --commission <id>    Commission/writ ID (required)
#   --repo <path>        Path to git repo (required)
#   --spec-file <path>   Commission spec (enables aware + integration scoring)
#   --base-commit <sha>  Override: start of commit range
#   --commit <sha>       Override: end of commit range
#   --output-dir <path>  Where to write artifacts
#   --dry-run            Print plan without executing
#   --blind-only         Only run spec-blind quality scorer
#   --aware-only         Only run spec-aware quality scorer
#   --integration-only   Only run codebase integration scorer
#   --quality-only       Run both quality scorers, skip integration
#   --no-integration     Skip integration scorer (default: run if spec provided)

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
RUNNER="$PROJECT_ROOT/packages/instruments/src/cli.ts"

# ── Parse arguments ──────────────────────────────────────────

COMMISSION=""
REPO=""
SPEC_FILE=""
BASE_COMMIT=""
COMMIT=""
OUTPUT_DIR=""
DRY_RUN=""
BLIND_ONLY=false
AWARE_ONLY=false
INTEGRATION_ONLY=false
QUALITY_ONLY=false
NO_INTEGRATION=false

while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
  case "$1" in
    --commission)        COMMISSION="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    --repo)              REPO="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    --spec-file)         SPEC_FILE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    --base-commit)       BASE_COMMIT="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    --commit)            COMMIT="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    --output-dir)        OUTPUT_DIR="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    --dry-run)           DRY_RUN="--dry-run"; shift ;;
    --blind-only)        BLIND_ONLY=true; shift ;;
    --aware-only)        AWARE_ONLY=true; shift ;;
    --integration-only)  INTEGRATION_ONLY=true; shift ;;
    --quality-only)      QUALITY_ONLY=true; shift ;;
    --no-integration)    NO_INTEGRATION=true; shift ;;
    *)                   echo "Unknown argument: $1" >&2; exit 1 ;;
  esac
done

if [[ -z "$COMMISSION" || -z "$REPO" ]]; then
  echo "Error: --commission and --repo are required" >&2
  exit 1
fi

OUTPUT_DIR="${OUTPUT_DIR:-$PROJECT_ROOT/experiments/data/commissions/${COMMISSION}}"

# Build common params
PARAMS=(--param "commission=$COMMISSION" --param "repo=$REPO")
[[ -n "$BASE_COMMIT" ]] && PARAMS+=(--param "base_commit=$BASE_COMMIT")
[[ -n "$COMMIT" ]] && PARAMS+=(--param "commit=$COMMIT")

NODE_CMD=(node --disable-warning=ExperimentalWarning --experimental-transform-types)
COMMON_ARGS=(--instrument-root "$PROJECT_ROOT/experiments/instruments" --output-dir "$OUTPUT_DIR")

# ── Determine which instruments to run ───────────────────────

RUN_BLIND=true
RUN_AWARE=true
RUN_INTEGRATION=true

if [[ "$BLIND_ONLY" == true ]]; then
  RUN_AWARE=false; RUN_INTEGRATION=false
elif [[ "$AWARE_ONLY" == true ]]; then
  RUN_BLIND=false; RUN_INTEGRATION=false
elif [[ "$INTEGRATION_ONLY" == true ]]; then
  RUN_BLIND=false; RUN_AWARE=false
elif [[ "$QUALITY_ONLY" == true ]]; then
  RUN_INTEGRATION=false
fi

if [[ "$NO_INTEGRATION" == true ]]; then
  RUN_INTEGRATION=false
fi

# Aware and integration require spec
if [[ -z "$SPEC_FILE" ]]; then
  if [[ "$RUN_AWARE" == true && "$AWARE_ONLY" != true && "$INTEGRATION_ONLY" != true ]]; then
    RUN_AWARE=false
  elif [[ "$AWARE_ONLY" == true || "$INTEGRATION_ONLY" == true ]]; then
    echo "Error: --aware-only and --integration-only require --spec-file" >&2
    exit 1
  fi
  RUN_INTEGRATION=false
fi

# ── Run blind ────────────────────────────────────────────────

if [[ "$RUN_BLIND" == true ]]; then
  echo "═══ Spec-Blind Quality Review ═══"
  echo ""
  "${NODE_CMD[@]}" "$RUNNER" \
    --instrument-name spec-blind-quality-scorer \
    "${COMMON_ARGS[@]}" \
    "${PARAMS[@]}" \
    $DRY_RUN
  echo ""
fi

# ── Run aware ────────────────────────────────────────────────

if [[ "$RUN_AWARE" == true ]]; then
  echo "═══ Spec-Aware Quality Review ═══"
  echo ""
  "${NODE_CMD[@]}" "$RUNNER" \
    --instrument-name spec-aware-quality-scorer \
    "${COMMON_ARGS[@]}" \
    "${PARAMS[@]}" \
    --param "spec_file=$SPEC_FILE" \
    $DRY_RUN
  echo ""
fi

# ── Run integration ──────────────────────────────────────────

if [[ "$RUN_INTEGRATION" == true ]]; then
  echo "═══ Codebase Integration Review ═══"
  echo ""
  "${NODE_CMD[@]}" "$RUNNER" \
    --instrument-name codebase-integration-scorer \
    "${COMMON_ARGS[@]}" \
    "${PARAMS[@]}" \
    --param "spec_file=$SPEC_FILE" \
    $DRY_RUN
  echo ""
fi

# ── Auto-commit & push results ──────────────────────────────

if [[ -z "$DRY_RUN" && -d "$OUTPUT_DIR/instruments" ]]; then
  echo "── Committing instrument results ──"
  cd "$PROJECT_ROOT"
  # Stage only this commission's instrument output
  git add "$OUTPUT_DIR/instruments/"
  # Only commit if there's something staged
  if ! git diff --cached --quiet; then
    GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Laboratory GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=laboratory@nexus.local \
    GIT_COMMITTER_NAME=Laboratory GIT_COMMITTER_EMAIL=laboratory@nexus.local \
    git commit -m "instrument results for $COMMISSION"
    git push && echo "  Pushed." || echo "  ⚠ Push failed (non-fatal)."
  else
    echo "  Nothing new to commit."
  fi
fi

file.content:
# Session Summary — 2026-04-07T231500

Session: 0021e09c-be0a-4dde-96b2-0e9b204150cd

## What we did

- **Resolved second nexus branch contamination.** Local `/workspace/nexus/` main had diverged from `origin/main` — 2 local-only commits, 2 remote-only commits. Traced `b1f3da0` (Artificer, commission `w-mnowtmnp`) as contamination from the kit-contributed-rig-templates commission. Rebased local onto origin, resolved two conflicts in `clerk.test.ts` and `spider.test.ts` (both: merge parameterizable kits/apparatuses with startupWarnings stub). Pushed clean linear history.
- **Fixed Oculus runtime ReferenceError.** Landing page and `/api/_status` handlers referenced `port` — a variable scoped inside `startServer()` — causing `ReferenceError: port is not defined` at runtime. Changed both references to `serverPort` (the closure-level variable).
- **Fixed 18 broken Oculus tests.** Six `describe` blocks from the `372be6f` landing page commission called `start()` but never `startServer()`, so every `fetch` hit ECONNREFUSED. Added the missing `startServer()` calls. All 69 oculus tests now passing.
- **Commits:** rebase resolution pushed as `abde39a`, oculus fix pushed as `6589dfc`.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Rebase over reset** — Sean correctly caught that `b1f3da0` was the only copy of the kit-contributed-rig-templates work (no remote branch, no worktree). Rebase preserved both local commits.
- **No vibers guild config update needed** — the port error was a code bug, not a config issue.

## Next steps & open questions

- **Container isolation** remains the structural fix for worktree contamination (second incident now). Todo at `.scratch/todo/session-directory-isolation.md`.
- **Commission `w-mnowtmnp` outcome** still null in commission-log — needs recording once sealed.
- **Oculus landing page commission (`372be6f`)** delivered code with two bugs (port scoping, missing startServer in tests). Possible X013/X014 data point on test quality in autonomous output.

### Notable moments

- Sean's correction: *"are you sure b1f3da0 isn't part of the work also?"* — Coco had assumed the contamination commit was duplicated on remote (as in the previous incident), but this time the local copy was the only copy. Would have lost the commission's work if we'd reset.

### Experiment observations

- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco resolved branch contamination (rebase + conflict resolution) and fixed two Oculus bugs — both directed by Sean. The Oculus fix is arguably commissionable (pure code fix, clear scope), but the context was "I'm seeing this error right now, fix it" — immediacy over process, same pattern as the startup tool last session.

content.text:
Still waiting on spec-aware. Let me give you my review while we wait.

---

## Review: w-mnoznabx-3f83f1ad2c50 — Parlour Oculus Page

**Overall: Solid work.** 1,947 lines across 9 files, 57 tests, clean build. The implement→review→revise pipeline did its job — the review caught real issues (variable shadowing, validation ordering) and the revise fixed them plus added 9 route handler tests.

**What went well:**
- **`routes.ts` is clean and well-structured.** Duck-typed `RouteContribution` avoids an Oculus import dependency. The `resolveCwd()` helper with graceful codexes fallback is exactly right.
- **SSE streaming via manual `ReadableStream` parsing** in `app.js` is correctly implemented — `EventSource` only supports GET, so this is the right approach. Buffer handling with `split(/\n\n/)` and keeping the trailing incomplete element is correct.
- **The `show()` enrichment** consolidates the existing session-fetch loop nicely — one pass through `sessionDocMap` serves both per-turn fields and the aggregate cost.
- **Test coverage** is good: 8 listRoles tests, 13 show-enrichment tests, 9 route handler integration tests. The revise session specifically added the route handler tests that the review flagged.
- **Lazy conversation creation** (R22) is correctly implemented — `conversation_created` SSE event only fires when the first message is sent without a `conversationId`.

**Nits (livable):**
- **Cost card shows `$0.0000` format** — 4 decimal places for USD is unusual. `$0.00` or `$X.XX` would be more readable. Minor CSS/template thing.
- **`innerHTML` for cost card** — using `innerHTML` with template literals that include computed values. The values are numeric (from JSON), not user input, so no XSS risk here. But it's a pattern worth noting — the rest of the page correctly uses `textContent` and DOM construction.
- **Conversation title refresh** makes a full `GET /api/parlour/conversations` call just to update one sidebar item's title. A bit heavy, but only fires once per turn completion, so not a real problem.
- **Empty `catch {}` blocks** throughout `app.js` — silently swallowing errors. Fine for a UI that's primarily a dev tool, but makes debugging harder.

**Failure analysis:**
The commission didn't fail from code quality — it failed because of a **rebase conflict at seal time**. Two commissions (`w-mnoznabx` Parlour page and `w-mnoznn5z` Loom page) both added `listRoles()` to the Loom. The Loom page commission sealed first, so when the Parlour page's seal tried to rebase, it hit conflicts in `loom.ts`, `loom.test.ts`, and `index.ts`. The seal engine correctly detected this and failed rather than producing a broken merge.

This is actually the system working as designed — concurrent commissions with overlapping scope should be caught at seal time. The gap is that there's no recovery path: a rebase conflict means the work is abandoned even when it's complete and correct. A human (me, just now) can resolve the conflicts trivially.

Let me check on the instruments...

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
# picture of how work is routed in the system.
#
# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
#
# Fields:
#   session        — Coco's Claude session ID (join key to archived transcripts)
#   date           — date the work was done (YYYY-MM-DD)
#   item           — short description of the work (one line)
#   commissionable — whether this work could have been an autonomous commission
#   justification  — why it can't be commissioned (required when commissionable is false)
#
# Justifications (required when commissionable: false):
#   interactive     — required real-time iteration with Sean
#   sanctum         — sanctum/experiment/meta work outside the commission boundary
#   decisional      — output was a decision, not an artifact

## Wrap-up: w-mnoznabx-3f83f1ad2c50 (Parlour Oculus Page)

### Why it failed
The **seal engine hit a rebase conflict**. Two concurrent commissions — this one (`w-mnoznabx`, Parlour page) and `w-mnoznn5z` (Loom page) — both independently added `listRoles()` to the Loom apparatus. The Loom page commission sealed first, so when the Parlour page's seal tried to rebase onto main, it hit conflicts in `loom.ts`, `loom.test.ts`, and `index.ts`. Seal correctly refused to proceed.

### What I did
1. ✅ **Cherry-picked** the two worktree commits onto main, resolving the Loom overlap (dropped duplicate `listRoles()` code, kept only parlour-specific changes)
2. ✅ **Verified** — 140 tests pass (83 loom + 57 parlour), clean build
3. ✅ **Pushed** to `origin/main`
4. ✅ **Cleaned** 640 build artifacts from `/workspace/nexus/` (agent ran `pnpm build` in the shared clone)
5. ✅ **Updated** writ and rig status to `completed` in the Stacks DB
6. ✅ **Updated** commission log: `outcome: success` with explanatory note
7. ✅ **Ran instruments**: blind **4.17**, aware **4.33**, integration partial (1/3 parsed at 4.00)

### Contamination
**None from this commission.** `/workspace/nexus/` is clean — `main` in sync with `origin/main`, zero untracked files.

### Systemic observation
This is the first **seal-time failure from concurrent commissions** with overlapping scope. The system detected it correctly (good!) but has no recovery path short of manual intervention (less good). Worth noting for the seal engine's future design — an interactive conflict resolution mode, or at minimum, a "try rebase, park if conflicts" behavior that doesn't mark the writ as `failed`.

content.text:
**There's the bug.** Here's the sequence:

1. Arbor starts the Oculus apparatus (`app.apparatus.start(startupCtx)` at line 196)
2. During `start()`, the Oculus scans `g.apparatuses()` (line 429) — this includes **all apparatuses that have already started** (they're in `orderedApparatuses` and their `provides` was registered)
3. If the Parlour started *before* the Oculus, it's already in `g.apparatuses()` → scanned once
4. Then Arbor fires `plugin:initialized` for each apparatus *after* its `start()` (line 204) — but the Oculus already subscribed to `plugin:initialized` during *its own* `start()`
5. When apparatuses that started *after* the Oculus fire `plugin:initialized`, they get scanned by the late-arriving handler — that's correct and not a dupe
6. **But**: `plugin:initialized` is also fired for apparatuses that started *before* the Oculus (they already fired at line 204 before the Oculus existed). Wait, no — the events fired *before* the Oculus subscribed, so the Oculus wouldn't see them.

Hmm, let me re-read the order more carefully. Apparatuses start in dependency order. The Oculus `consumes: ['tools']`. The Parlour has no dependency on Oculus. So the startup order depends on topology...

content.text:
Oculus `requires: ['tools']` but `consumes: ['pages', 'routes']`. The `consumes` relation means it needs them available but doesn't enforce startup order. The Parlour `requires: ['stacks', 'animator', 'loom']` — no dependency on Oculus.

So the startup order is determined by the topological sort. The Parlour might start before or after the Oculus depending on the dependency graph. If the Parlour starts **after** the Oculus:

1. Oculus starts → scans existing apparatuses (Parlour NOT yet started) → subscribes to `plugin:initialized`
2. Parlour starts → `plugin:initialized` fires → Oculus's handler scans it → **registered once** ✅

If the Parlour starts **before** the Oculus:

1. Parlour starts → `plugin:initialized` fires (Oculus not listening yet)
2. Oculus starts → scans existing apparatuses (Parlour IS in `g.apparatuses()`) → **registered once** via scan
3. Parlour's `plugin:initialized` already fired, Oculus missed it → **still one** ✅

That logic looks correct — only one registration either way. Let me check if maybe the Oculus itself is seeing `plugin:initialized` for the Parlour AFTER also having scanned it. That would happen if the Parlour starts after the Oculus.

Wait — I need to look more carefully at when `plugin:initialized` fires relative to when things appear in `g.apparatuses()`. Let me re-read arbor.ts:

Line 196: `await app.apparatus.start(startupCtx)` — Oculus starts here, scans `g.apparatuses()`.
But `orderedApparatuses` is the full list — `g.apparatuses()` returns `[...orderedApparatuses]` at line 176, which includes ALL ordered apparatuses, including ones that haven't started yet!

🎯 **There it is.** Line 176: `apparatuses()` returns `[...orderedApparatuses]` — the **full ordered list**, including apparatuses that haven't started yet. It's the topologically sorted array, not a "started so far" list.

So here's the double-registration sequence:

1. Arbor builds the full `orderedApparatuses` list
2. Starts looping: Stacks starts, Animator starts, Loom starts, etc.
3. **Parlour starts** → `plugin:initialized` fires → Oculus hasn't started yet, nobody listening → fine
4. **Oculus starts** → calls `g.apparatuses()` → gets ALL apparatuses including Parlour → **scans Parlour, registers page**
5. Oculus subscribes to `plugin:initialized`
6. Next apparatus in the loop starts → fires `plugin:initialized` → Oculus handler fires
7. ...eventually the loop gets to apparatus that started *after* the Oculus... 

Wait, the Oculus already started in step 4. After step 4, the next apparatus starts and fires `plugin:initialized`. That apparatus might NOT be the Parlour — the Parlour already fired at step 3.

So actually, the Parlour should only be registered once (in step 4 via the initial scan). It won't fire `plugin:initialized` again.

Unless... the Parlour starts *after* the Oculus. Then:

1. **Oculus starts** → scans `g.apparatuses()` → gets ALL including Parlour (which hasn't started yet!) → **scans Parlour, registers page** ← but is the supportKit populated before `start()` is called?
2. Oculus subscribes to `plugin:initialized`
3. **Parlour starts** → `plugin:initialized` fires → Oculus handler → **scans Parlour again, registers page** → **DUPLICATE!**

That's the bug! `g.apparatuses()` returns ALL ordered apparatuses, including ones that haven't started yet. The Oculus scans them all during its `start()`. Then when those not-yet-started apparatuses eventually start and fire `plugin:initialized`, the Oculus handler scans them *again*.

The `supportKit` is a static property on the apparatus object (defined in the plugin factory, not populated in `start()`), so it's available even before `start()` is called.

Sean, here's the bug:

---

## Two Parlour Pages: Root Cause

**`g.apparatuses()` returns all topologically-sorted apparatuses, including ones that haven't started yet.** 

The Oculus does two things in `start()`:
1. Scans `g.apparatuses()` — picks up ALL apparatuses including not-yet-started ones (their `supportKit` is already defined as a static property)
2. Subscribes to `plugin:initialized` — picks up each apparatus again when it actually starts

Any apparatus that starts **after** the Oculus gets its page registered **twice**: once from the initial `g.apparatuses()` scan, and again from the `plugin:initialized` handler.

**Fix options:**
1. **Dedup in `registerPage()`** — check if `pages` already has an entry with the same `id` before pushing. Quick, defensive.
2. **Fix in Arbor** — make `apparatuses()` only return apparatuses that have actually started. More correct but bigger blast radius.
3. **Filter in the Oculus scan** — only scan apparatuses that have `provides` registered (a proxy for "has started"). Hacky.

Option 1 is the right immediate fix — it's a one-liner and protects against any other dedup issues. Want me to commission it or just fix it directly?

message.content: can you think through a better fix for this globally, since its a pretty common pattern and getting more common... draft a brief that we can send over to fix

content.text:
Good call — this is a systemic issue, not just an Oculus one. Let me think through the design space.

The **scan + subscribe** pattern appears in at least six apparatuses now:

| Apparatus | What it scans for |
|---|---|
| Oculus | pages, routes, tools |
| Instrumentarium | tools |
| Fabricator | engine designs |
| Loom | kit roles |
| Spider | rig templates, block types, engines |
| Clerk | writ types |

Every one of them does the same thing: scan `g.apparatuses()` at startup, then subscribe to `plugin:initialized` for late arrivals. And every one of them is vulnerable to the same double-registration bug — it just manifests visibly in the Oculus because pages appear in a nav bar.

The root problem is **`g.apparatuses()` doesn't distinguish between "loaded and topologically sorted" and "actually started."** It returns the full ordered array, which includes apparatuses whose `start()` hasn't been called yet. So the contract is ambiguous — callers think they're getting "things that exist and are running" but they're actually getting "things that will eventually exist."

**Option A: Fix `g.apparatuses()` to only return started apparatuses.**
One change in Arbor — maintain a `startedApparatuses` array that grows as each `start()` completes. `g.apparatuses()` returns that instead of the full sorted list. The scan+subscribe pattern becomes correct by construction: scan catches everything before you, subscribe catches everything after you, zero overlap. Clean, minimal blast radius.

**Option B: Dedup in every consumer.**
Each apparatus adds a `Set` of already-scanned plugin IDs. Fixes the symptom everywhere but doesn't fix the contract. Every new apparatus has to remember to dedup.

**Option C: Introduce a "started" event distinct from "initialized".**
More complex, more moving parts, doesn't solve the `g.apparatuses()` contract issue.

Option A is clearly right. Let me draft it.

input.content:
# Fix: `g.apparatuses()` Returns Unstarted Apparatuses

## Problem

`guild().apparatuses()` returns all topologically-sorted apparatuses, including ones whose `start()` has not yet been called. This breaks the scan+subscribe pattern used by six apparatuses (Oculus, Instrumentarium, Fabricator, Loom, Spider, Clerk), causing double-registration of contributions from apparatuses that start after the scanning apparatus.

**Visible symptom:** Duplicate pages in the Oculus navigation bar (e.g., two "Parlour" links).

**Root cause in Arbor:** `apparatuses()` at line 176 of `arbor.ts` returns `[...orderedApparatuses]` — the full sorted array, populated during plugin loading, before any `start()` calls.

**Why this matters now:** The scan+subscribe pattern is the standard way apparatuses discover contributions from other apparatuses. It's used in six places and growing. Every instance is vulnerable to this bug.

## The Scan+Subscribe Pattern

```typescript
// During start():
// 1. Scan existing apparatuses for contributions
for (const app of g.apparatuses()) {
  scanApparatus(app);  // picks up supportKit.pages, .tools, .engines, etc.
}

// 2. Subscribe for late-arriving apparatuses
ctx.on('plugin:initialized', (plugin) => {
  if ('apparatus' in plugin) scanApparatus(plugin);
});
```

The contract assumption is:
- **Scan** catches everything that started before me
- **Subscribe** catches everything that starts after me
- **No overlap**

But `g.apparatuses()` returns unstarted apparatuses too, so scan overlaps with subscribe for any apparatus that starts after the scanner.

## Fix

Maintain a separate `startedApparatuses` array in `arbor.ts` that grows as each apparatus completes `start()`. Return that from `g.apparatuses()` instead of `orderedApparatuses`.

### Changes in `packages/framework/arbor/src/arbor.ts`

Add a new array alongside the existing structures:

```typescript
const startedApparatuses: LoadedApparatus[] = [];
```

Update the guild instance to return it:

```typescript
apparatuses() { return [...startedApparatuses]; },
```

In the startup loop, push to `startedApparatuses` after each `start()` completes (before firing `plugin:initialized`):

```typescript
await app.apparatus.start(startupCtx);

// ... existing provides re-check ...

startedApparatuses.push(app);
await fireEvent(eventHandlers, 'plugin:initialized', app);
```

This ensures:
- When apparatus X calls `g.apparatuses()` during its `start()`, it only sees apparatuses that have already completed their `start()`
- When `plugin:initialized` fires for apparatus Y (which starts after X), Y is NOT in the list X already scanned
- The scan+subscribe pattern produces exactly one registration per contribution

### Test changes in `packages/framework/arbor/src/arbor.test.ts`

Add a test that verifies `g.apparatuses()` only returns started apparatuses during `start()`:

1. Create two apparatuses A and B where B depends on A (so A starts first)
2. In B's `start()`, call `g.apparatuses()` and record the result
3. Verify A is in the list but B is not
4. After startup completes, verify both A and B are in `g.apparatuses()`

Add a test that verifies no double-delivery:

1. Create apparatus X that scans `g.apparatuses()` and subscribes to `plugin:initialized`, recording every apparatus it sees
2. Create apparatus Y that starts after X
3. Verify Y appears exactly once in X's records (via `plugin:initialized`, not via the initial scan)

## Validation

- V2: New test confirms `g.apparatuses()` excludes not-yet-started apparatuses during `start()`
- V3: New test confirms scan+subscribe produces exactly one registration per apparatus
- V4: Oculus shows each page exactly once in the nav bar (manual verification)

## Risk

Low. The only behavioral change is that `g.apparatuses()` returns fewer items during the startup window. After all apparatuses have started, it returns the same full list as before. Any code that calls `g.apparatuses()` outside of `start()` (e.g., in route handlers, tool handlers) sees no difference.

# Fix: `g.apparatuses()` Returns Unstarted Apparatuses

## Problem

`guild().apparatuses()` returns all topologically-sorted apparatuses, including ones whose `start()` has not yet been called. This breaks the scan+subscribe pattern used by six apparatuses (Oculus, Instrumentarium, Fabricator, Loom, Spider, Clerk), causing double-registration of contributions from apparatuses that start after the scanning apparatus.

**Visible symptom:** Duplicate pages in the Oculus navigation bar (e.g., two "Parlour" links).

**Root cause in Arbor:** `apparatuses()` at line 176 of `arbor.ts` returns `[...orderedApparatuses]` — the full sorted array, populated during plugin loading, before any `start()` calls.

**Why this matters now:** The scan+subscribe pattern is the standard way apparatuses discover contributions from other apparatuses. It's used in six places and growing. Every instance is vulnerable to this bug.

## The Scan+Subscribe Pattern

```typescript
// During start():
// 1. Scan existing apparatuses for contributions
for (const app of g.apparatuses()) {
  scanApparatus(app);  // picks up supportKit.pages, .tools, .engines, etc.
}

// 2. Subscribe for late-arriving apparatuses
ctx.on('plugin:initialized', (plugin) => {
  if ('apparatus' in plugin) scanApparatus(plugin);
});
```

The contract assumption is:
- **Scan** catches everything that started before me
- **Subscribe** catches everything that starts after me
- **No overlap**

But `g.apparatuses()` returns unstarted apparatuses too, so scan overlaps with subscribe for any apparatus that starts after the scanner.

## Fix

Maintain a separate `startedApparatuses` array in `arbor.ts` that grows as each apparatus completes `start()`. Return that from `g.apparatuses()` instead of `orderedApparatuses`.

### Changes in `packages/framework/arbor/src/arbor.ts`

Add a new array alongside the existing structures:

```typescript
const startedApparatuses: LoadedApparatus[] = [];
```

Update the guild instance to return it:

```typescript
apparatuses() { return [...startedApparatuses]; },
```

In the startup loop, push to `startedApparatuses` after each `start()` completes (before firing `plugin:initialized`):

```typescript
await app.apparatus.start(startupCtx);

// ... existing provides re-check ...

startedApparatuses.push(app);
await fireEvent(eventHandlers, 'plugin:initialized', app);
```

This ensures:
- When apparatus X calls `g.apparatuses()` during its `start()`, it only sees apparatuses that have already completed their `start()`
- When `plugin:initialized` fires for apparatus Y (which starts after X), Y is NOT in the list X already scanned
- The scan+subscribe pattern produces exactly one registration per contribution

### Test changes in `packages/framework/arbor/src/arbor.test.ts`

Add a test that verifies `g.apparatuses()` only returns started apparatuses during `start()`:

1. Create two apparatuses A and B where B depends on A (so A starts first)
2. In B's `start()`, call `g.apparatuses()` and record the result
3. Verify A is in the list but B is not
4. After startup completes, verify both A and B are in `g.apparatuses()`

Add a test that verifies no double-delivery:

1. Create apparatus X that scans `g.apparatuses()` and subscribes to `plugin:initialized`, recording every apparatus it sees
2. Create apparatus Y that starts after X
3. Verify Y appears exactly once in X's records (via `plugin:initialized`, not via the initial scan)

## Validation

- V1: All existing arbor tests pass (the change is backward-compatible for post-startup callers)
- V2: New test confirms `g.apparatuses()` excludes not-yet-started apparatuses during `start()`
- V3: New test confirms scan+subscribe produces exactly one registration per apparatus
- V4: Oculus shows each page exactly once in the nav bar (manual verification)

## Risk

Low. The only behavioral change is that `g.apparatuses()` returns fewer items during the startup window. After all apparatuses have started, it returns the same full list as before. Any code that calls `g.apparatuses()` outside of `start()` (e.g., in route handlers, tool handlers) sees no difference.

Code that relied on seeing unstarted apparatuses during `start()` was already buggy — it was reading `supportKit` from apparatuses that hadn't initialized their state yet.

Draft's in `.scratch/`. Here's the short version:

---

**The fix is in Arbor, not in the consumers.** `g.apparatuses()` should return only apparatuses that have completed `start()`, not the full topologically-sorted list. One new array (`startedApparatuses`), one line change in the guild instance, one `push()` in the startup loop. The scan+subscribe pattern becomes correct by construction — scan catches everything before you, subscribe catches everything after you, zero overlap. No consumer changes needed.

It's a complexity-1, maybe complexity-2 commission. The brief's at `.scratch/fix-apparatuses-returns-unstarted.md` — want me to send it over?

message.content: is scan+subscrib really the best way to do this?

content.text:
Hmm. Good question. Let me actually think about this rather than just patching the existing pattern.

The scan+subscribe pattern exists because apparatuses start sequentially and need to discover contributions from other apparatuses. But it's fundamentally a workaround for **"I don't know what order things start in, so I'll look at what's already here and then watch for what comes later."**

That's two code paths doing the same thing. Every consumer implements it independently. And as we just saw, getting the boundary between "already here" and "coming later" wrong causes bugs.

**What if there was only one path?**

The real question is: *why does an apparatus need to discover contributions during `start()` at all?* The contributions (supportKit pages, tools, engines, etc.) are **statically declared** — they're properties on the plugin object, not computed during `start()`. Arbor already has all the plugins loaded and sorted before any `start()` is called.

So the options are:

**1. Arbor collects all contributions before any `start()`, passes them in via `StartupContext`**

Instead of each apparatus scanning for itself, Arbor does one pass over all plugins, groups contributions by type, and hands them to the relevant apparatus:

```typescript
// Arbor, before start loop:
const allPages = collectAll(plugins, 'pages');
const allTools = collectAll(plugins, 'tools');
const allEngines = collectAll(plugins, 'engines');

// During start:
ctx.contributions.pages  // available to Oculus
ctx.contributions.tools  // available to Instrumentarium
```

No scan, no subscribe, no timing issues. But this means Arbor needs to know the contribution taxonomy, which feels like coupling that shouldn't live there. Arbor is generic — it doesn't know what a "page" or an "engine" is.

**2. Arbor fires a single `contributions-ready` event after all plugins have started**

All apparatuses subscribe during `start()`, but nobody scans eagerly. After the last `start()` completes, Arbor fires one event and every apparatus processes the full set at once:

```typescript
ctx.on('contributions:ready', () => {
  for (const app of g.apparatuses()) {  // now safe — all started
    scanApparatus(app);
  }
});
```

One code path. No timing ambiguity. But it means no apparatus can use contributions *during* its own `start()` — everything is deferred to post-startup. Is that OK? Looking at the consumers:

- **Oculus** registers routes and pages → could defer, routes only matter when the server starts (which is itself deferred to an explicit tool call)
- **Instrumentarium** collects tools → tools are only resolved at weave/session time, not during `start()`
- **Fabricator** collects engine designs → only used when a rig is created
- **Loom** collects kit roles → only used at weave time
- **Spider** collects rig templates, block types → only used at rig creation
- **Clerk** collects writ types → only used at writ creation

None of them actually *need* contributions during `start()`. They all collect eagerly but use lazily. So deferring to a post-startup event works fine.

**3. Lazy pull instead of eager push**

Instead of scanning at all, each apparatus queries contributions on demand:

```typescript
// Oculus, when building the nav:
const pages = g.apparatuses()
  .flatMap(a => (a.apparatus.supportKit as OculusKit)?.pages ?? []);
```

No registration, no event, no cache. Just read the source of truth every time. Simple, but:
- Every consumer has to know the `supportKit` shape
- Performance cost if called frequently (probably negligible)
- Loses the ability to do one-time validation/transformation at registration

**My take:**

Option 2 is the sweet spot. It preserves the existing registration pattern (apparatuses still own their scanning logic) but eliminates the timing bug structurally. One event, one scan pass, no overlap possible. The change in Arbor is tiny — fire one new event after the startup loop — and the change in each consumer is *removing* code (delete the `g.apparatuses()` scan from `start()`, keep only the event handler, rename it from `plugin:initialized` to `contributions:ready` or whatever).

Actually — even simpler. What if `plugin:initialized` just isn't fired until *all* apparatuses have started? Right now it fires incrementally (after each `start()`). If it fired in a batch at the end, the existing subscribe-only code would work unchanged — just delete the scan.

But that breaks any apparatus that needs to react to *specific* plugins as they start (e.g., the Loom registering kit roles needs to know which plugin contributed them). The incremental `plugin:initialized` carries the identity of the plugin.

So we probably want both:
- Keep `plugin:initialized` firing incrementally (for cases that need per-plugin identity)
- Add `startup:complete` that fires once after all plugins are started
- Consumers move their bulk scanning to `startup:complete` and use `plugin:initialized` only for dynamic late-arriving plugins (if we ever have hot-loading)

Though... we don't have hot-loading. Every plugin is known at startup. `plugin:initialized` only fires during the startup loop. So right now, `startup:complete` fully replaces the scan+subscribe pattern.

Want me to revise the brief around option 2 (`startup:complete` event, delete the scan half of scan+subscribe)?

## Problem

`guild().apparatuses()` returns all topologically-sorted apparatuses, including ones whose `start()` has not yet been called. This breaks the scan+subscribe pattern used by six apparatuses (Oculus, Instrumentarium, Fabricator, Loom, Spider, Clerk), causing double-registration of contributions from apparatuses that start after the scanning apparatus.

**Why this matters now:** The scan+subscribe pattern is the standard way apparatuses discover contributions from other apparatuses. It's used in six places and growing. Every instance is vulnerable to this bug.

## The Scan+Subscribe Pattern

```typescript

// 1. Scan existing apparatuses for contributions
for (const app of g.apparatuses()) {
  scanApparatus(app);  // picks up supportKit.pages, .tools, .engines, etc.
}

// 2. Subscribe for late-arriving apparatuses

  if ('apparatus' in plugin) scanApparatus(plugin);
```

The contract assumption is:
- **Scan** catches everything that started before me
- **Subscribe** catches everything that starts after me

But `g.apparatuses()` returns unstarted apparatuses too, so scan overlaps with subscribe for any apparatus that starts after the scanner.

```typescript
apparatuses() { return [...startedApparatuses]; },
```

```typescript
await app.apparatus.start(startupCtx);

// ... existing provides re-check ...

await fireEvent(eventHandlers, 'plugin:initialized', app);
```

content.text:
Draft's at `.scratch/fix-apparatuses-returns-unstarted.md`. Here's the core idea:

**SupportKit contributions are static declarations — they don't need `start()` to be readable.** So pull contribution wiring out of `start()` entirely. Arbor collects all `supportKit.*` entries into a `ContributionRegistry` *before* any apparatus starts, then exposes it on `StartupContext`. Each consumer replaces its scan+subscribe with one `ctx.contributions('pages')` call.

Three lifecycle phases: **Load → Wire → Start.** Wire is new. `plugin:initialized` stops being the contribution discovery mechanism and becomes `apparatus:started` — just a notification that a specific apparatus finished starting, for cases that actually need that (CDC watcher setup, etc.).

The `ContributionRegistry` is generic — Arbor doesn't know what a "page" or "engine" is. It just indexes `supportKit` entries by key, tagged with source plugin ID and package name. Each consumer interprets their own contribution type.

Estimated complexity 8 — Arbor core change is small but it touches six apparatus consumers. Each consumer change is mechanical (delete scan+subscribe, add `ctx.contributions()` call).

I notice you also updated the commission log — `outcome: partial`, `revision_required: true`, with a note about `turnRoute` failing to forward the user message to `takeTurnStreaming`. Nice catch! That's a real bug — the anima would get an empty prompt on every turn. Want me to look at that fix or is it already handled?

message.content: already handled... back to this, are we getting both plain old kit plugins and 'supportKits'? Maybe instead of a 'contribution registry', could the arbor guild just expose all 'kits', merging the two types? or is contribution registry jsut the merge of this? do we need any docs for kit authors about what they cant do in their 'static declarations', since its technical typescript cdode and not like json?

content.text:
Great questions. Let me untangle these.

**Are kits and supportKits the same thing?** Basically yes. A kit plugin is a standalone contribution bundle — it has no `start()`, no lifecycle, it's just a bag of tools/pages/roles/etc. A supportKit is the same bag, but attached to an apparatus as a sidecar. The Stacks apparatus loads them identically — it doesn't care whether the contributions came from a kit plugin or an apparatus's supportKit.

So yeah — the "contribution registry" is really just "all kits, unified." The distinction between `kit` and `supportKit` is about *packaging* (standalone plugin vs apparatus sidecar), not about what's inside. Arbor could expose a single `g.kits()` that merges both, tagged with their source.

Actually, wait. There's a reason they're separate today. Kit plugins are loaded and processed *before* any apparatus starts (line 182-185 in arbor.ts — kits get `plugin:initialized` first). SupportKits are attached to apparatuses. The current `g.kits()` returns only standalone kit plugins. If we merge them, `g.kits()` returns everything — which is exactly what consumers want.

**So the proposal simplifies to:** Arbor collects all kit contributions (from both kit plugins and apparatus supportKits) into a single list during the Wire phase. `g.kits()` returns the merged set, or there's a `ctx.contributions('pages')` query that flattens across all of them. Either way, consumers don't care about the kit-vs-supportKit distinction.

**On static declarations and kit author guidance —** you're right to flag this. SupportKit values today are *mostly* static, but they're TypeScript objects, not JSON. A supportKit could technically do:

```typescript
supportKit: {
  tools: [buildToolDynamically()],  // function call at module load time
  pages: computePages(),            // depends on... what?
}
```

Module-load-time side effects are fine (that's how all the tool definitions work — `tool({...})` is a function call that returns a descriptor). The constraint is: **supportKit must not depend on `start()` having been called**, because Wire happens before Start. Specifically:
- No calling `guild()` (singleton isn't set yet during Wire)
- No reading guild config
- No accessing other apparatuses' `provides`
- No filesystem access relative to guild home

Tool *handlers* can do all of that (they run at invocation time, long after startup). But the tool *definition* — name, params, description, permission — must be static.

Is this actually a concern in practice? Looking at all existing supportKits... every single one is a static object literal or an imported constant. Nobody's computing supportKit entries dynamically. The tool handlers are closures that capture the apparatus's internal state, but the definitions themselves are plain data.

I think the guidance is simple enough to be a one-paragraph note in the kit authoring doc rather than a formal constraint. Something like: *"supportKit is read during the Wire phase, before any apparatus starts. Definitions must be self-contained — no guild(), no config, no apparatus state. Handler functions run later and have full access."*

Let me revise the brief to fold these in — merge kits and supportKits, drop the "ContributionRegistry" as a separate concept, and add the kit authoring note.

7	Deeper issue: contribution wiring and apparatus startup are tangled together. SupportKit declarations are static (no `start()` needed to read them), but every consumer scans them during `start()` and subscribes to `plugin:initialized` for late arrivals. Two code paths doing the same thing, with a timing-dependent boundary between them.
8	
9	## Design: Explicit Lifecycle Phases
10	
11	Arbor's startup becomes three explicit phases:
12	
13	```
14	LOAD  →  WIRE  →  START
15	          ↑         ↑
16	       phase event  phase event
17	```
18	
19	**Load** (unchanged): Plugins are discovered, validated, topologically sorted.
20	
21	**Wire** (new): Arbor iterates all plugins and collects every `supportKit` entry into a central `ContributionRegistry`. Fires `phase:wired` when complete. No apparatus `start()` has been called yet.
22	
23	**Start** (refined): Each apparatus runs `start()` in dependency order. Contributions are already fully available via `ctx.contributions()`. Fires `phase:started` when all apparatuses have completed `start()`.
24	
25	### ContributionRegistry
26	
27	A simple collect-and-query structure owned by Arbor. It doesn't interpret contribution types — it just indexes `supportKit.*` entries by key, tagged with their source plugin.
28	
29	```typescript
30	interface ContributionEntry<T = unknown> {
31	  pluginId: string;
32	  packageName: string;
33	  value: T;
34	}
35	
36	interface ContributionRegistry {
37	  /** Get all contributions for a given supportKit key. */
38	  get<T>(key: string): ContributionEntry<T>[];
39	}
40	```
41	
42	During the Wire phase, Arbor collects:
43	
44	```typescript
45	for (const plugin of [...kits, ...orderedApparatuses]) {
46	  const kit = 'kit' in plugin ? plugin.kit : plugin.apparatus.supportKit;
47	  if (!kit || typeof kit !== 'object') continue;
48	
49	  for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(kit)) {
50	    // Skip 'books' — already handled specially by Stacks
51	    registry.add(key, plugin.id, plugin.packageName, value);
52	  }
53	}
54	```
55	
56	The registry is exposed on `StartupContext`:
57	
58	```typescript
59	interface StartupContext {
60	  on(event: string, handler: Function): void;
61	  contributions: ContributionRegistry;
62	}
63	```
64	
65	### Consumer Changes
66	
67	Each apparatus replaces its scan+subscribe with a single `ctx.contributions()` call during `start()`.
68	
69	**Oculus** (pages + routes):
70	
71	```typescript
72	// Before (scan+subscribe):
73	for (const app of g.apparatuses()) { scanApparatus(app); }
74	ctx.on('plugin:initialized', (p) => { if ('apparatus' in p) scanApparatus(p); });
75	
76	// After (single query):
77	for (const entry of ctx.contributions<PageContribution[]>('pages')) {
78	  for (const page of entry.value) {
79	    registerPage(page, resolveDirForPackage(entry.packageName, page.dir));
80	  }
81	}
82	for (const entry of ctx.contributions<RouteContribution[]>('routes')) {
83	  for (const route of entry.value) {
84	    registerCustomRoute(route, entry.pluginId);
85	  }
86	}
87	```
88	
89	**Instrumentarium** (tools):
90	
91	```typescript
92	// Before:
93	for (const app of g.apparatuses()) {
94	  if (app.apparatus.supportKit) this.registerToolsFromKit(app.id, app.packageName, app.apparatus.supportKit);
95	}
96	ctx.on('plugin:initialized', (p) => { ... });
97	
98	// After:
99	for (const entry of ctx.contributions<AnyTool[]>('tools')) {
100	  for (const tool of entry.value) {
101	    this.registerTool(entry.pluginId, tool);
102	  }
103	}
104	```
105	
106	Same pattern for Fabricator (engines), Loom (roles), Spider (rig templates, block types, engines), Clerk (writ types).
107	
108	### `plugin:initialized` — Keep or Remove?
109	
110	Keep it, but clarify its purpose. It is NOT for contribution discovery (that's the Wire phase). It's for apparatus-to-apparatus notifications where one apparatus needs to react to another apparatus completing `start()` — e.g., registering CDC watchers on another apparatus's Stacks books.
111	
112	Rename to `apparatus:started` to clarify semantics. Kit equivalent: `kit:loaded` (fires during Wire phase).
113	
114	### Phase Events
115	
116	```typescript
117	ctx.on('phase:wired', () => { ... });    // all contributions collected
118	ctx.on('phase:started', () => { ... });  // all apparatuses started, guild operational
119	```
120	
121	These are useful for apparatuses that need to do post-startup work (e.g., the Oculus could defer route registration to `phase:wired` if it wanted, though querying `ctx.contributions` during `start()` is cleaner).
122	
123	## Requirements
124	
125	- R1: Arbor must collect all `supportKit` entries from all plugins into a `ContributionRegistry` before calling any apparatus `start()`.
126	- R2: `StartupContext` must expose `contributions: ContributionRegistry` so apparatuses can query contributions during `start()`.
127	- R3: `ContributionRegistry.get<T>(key)` must return all entries for a given key, each tagged with `pluginId` and `packageName`.
128	- R4: The Wire phase must handle both kit contributions (from `plugin.kit`) and apparatus contributions (from `plugin.apparatus.supportKit`).
129	- R5: `g.apparatuses()` must only return apparatuses that have completed `start()`.
130	- R6: Rename `plugin:initialized` to `apparatus:started` for apparatuses and `kit:loaded` for kits. Maintain backward compat by firing both old and new event names for one release cycle (log a deprecation warning on old name).
131	- R7: Oculus must replace its scan+subscribe pattern with `ctx.contributions('pages')` and `ctx.contributions('routes')`.
132	- R8: Instrumentarium must replace its scan+subscribe with `ctx.contributions('tools')`.
133	- R9: Fabricator must replace its scan+subscribe with `ctx.contributions('engines')`.
134	- R10: Loom must replace its scan+subscribe with `ctx.contributions('roles')`.
135	- R11: Spider must replace its scan+subscribe with `ctx.contributions('engines')`, `ctx.contributions('rigTemplates')`, and `ctx.contributions('blockTypes')`.
136	- R12: Clerk must replace its scan+subscribe with `ctx.contributions('writTypes')`.
137	- R13: All existing tests must pass after the refactor.
138	- R14: New tests must verify: (a) contributions are available during `start()`, (b) `g.apparatuses()` excludes unstarted apparatuses, (c) no double-registration occurs for any contribution type.
139	
140	## Scope Note
141	
142	R5 (fix `g.apparatuses()`) is included because it's trivial and eliminates the root cause even for any code that doesn't adopt the new pattern. But the main thrust is R1–R4 (the Wire phase) which makes the fix structural.
143	
144	Books are deliberately excluded from the contribution registry — they're already handled by Stacks through its own mechanism. The registry handles: tools, pages, routes, engines, roles, rigTemplates, blockTypes, writTypes.
145	
146	## Complexity
147	
148	Estimated 8. Touches Arbor (core framework) plus six apparatus plugins. Each apparatus change is mechanical (replace scan+subscribe with `ctx.contributions()`), but the breadth is wide and the Arbor changes need careful test coverage.
149

content.text:
Revised. The key changes:

- **Dropped `ContributionRegistry` as a separate concept.** It's just `ctx.kits(type)` — a flat query over unified kit entries. Arbor collects, consumers query, same idea but simpler vocabulary.
- **Kit = kit.** Whether standalone plugin or apparatus sidecar, a contribution bag is a contribution bag. Arbor merges them during Wire, consumers don't care about the distinction.
- **Kit authoring constraint documented.** One paragraph: definitions must be self-contained (no `guild()`, no config, no apparatus calls). Handlers are fine — they run at invocation time. In practice nobody violates this today, but it should be written down.
- **`g.kits()` stays as-is** (standalone kit plugins only). `ctx.kits(type)` is the new unified query. Different questions, different methods.

The brief's ready at `.scratch/fix-apparatuses-returns-unstarted.md`. Want me to commission it?
